schema.description: UHN ICF Automation Pipeline — canonical template registry.

symbol_guide.{{placeholder}}: REQUIRED substitution. Replace the entire {{...}} token with study-specific information extracted from the protocol.
symbol_guide.{{option1/option2}}: CHOOSE ONE. Pick the applicable option from the slash-separated list inside the braces (e.g., {{will/may}}, {{you/they}}, {{study/clinical trial}}).
symbol_guide.<Condition label>: CONDITIONAL BLOCK (single angle brackets). Include the text that follows this marker only if the stated condition applies to the study. Typically used for a single-paragraph or single-sentence addition (e.g., <If time permits>, <For single-blind studies>).
symbol_guide.<<Condition block>>: CONDITIONAL BLOCK (double angle brackets). Same meaning as <...> but conventionally marks a larger or more distinct scenario block, often with mutually exclusive alternatives (e.g., <<For single arm studies>>, <<If data sent outside Canada>>). Treat identically to <...> for extraction purposes.
symbol_guide.OR (standalone line): ALTERNATIVE. Choose exactly one of the blocks immediately above or below this marker — do not include both.
symbol_guide.• or - (list markers): BULLET POINT. Both markers are used interchangeably as list items.

field_guide.heading: UPPERCASE section heading as it appears in the ICF template.
field_guide.sub_section: Sub-section name, or null if none.
field_guide.required: true = must be included; false = include only if relevant to this study.
field_guide.complexity: Classification tags indicating extraction difficulty and protocol availability. Drives the RLM iteration budget and availability flags.
field_guide.is_in_protocol: true if the information is expected to be findable in the study protocol.
field_guide.partially_in_protocol: true if only some fields can be extracted from the protocol; the rest require manual entry by the study team.
field_guide.is_standard_text: true if required_text is boilerplate that can be used verbatim — no extraction needed.
field_guide.instructions: Author-facing directions for writing this ICF section. Not participant-facing. Used to guide LLM extraction.
field_guide.required_text: Mandated ICF wording. Contains {{placeholders}} to be filled in. Empty string if the section uses suggested_text only.
field_guide.suggested_text: Sample ICF language to use as a starting point. Contains <conditions>, <<block conditions>>, {{placeholders}}, and OR alternatives. Not required wording — adapt to the study.
field_guide.suggested_text_format: 'text' (default) or 'html' — set to 'html' and use an HTML string in suggested_text when table content is needed (e.g., section 13.6).
field_guide.adaptation_notes: Runtime-only field. Set by the dynamic adaptation pass to inject study-specific context into extraction prompts. Always null in the base registry; never serialised back to this file.

sections.heading: Informed Consent Form for Participation in a Research Study
sections.instructions: Insert study title as written on the protocol. Should the title be complicated, a title in simplified lay terms could be included after the full title of the study.

sections.heading: Informed Consent Form for Participation in a Research Study
sections.instructions: Insert sponsor’s study ID if applicable

sections.heading: Informed Consent Form for Participation in a Research Study
complexity: Not in protocol - requires manual entry
sections.instructions:
Insert name, department and telephone or pager number.
Indicate “Dr.” only for doctors licensed to practice medicine in Canada; indicate “Nurse” only for nurses licensed to practice in Canada. All other Investigators should be referred by their credentials and, if applicable, country of practice

sections.heading: Informed Consent Form for Participation in a Research Study
complexity: Not in protocol - requires manual entry
sections.instructions: Listing Co-Investigators is discouraged, but should be considered for studies involving expertise from different departments (e.g., multimodal studies).

sections.heading: Informed Consent Form for Participation in a Research Study
complexity: Not in protocol - requires manual entry
sections.instructions: Insert the name of the Sponsor or, if applicable, the funder(s) of the research as documented in the protocol and/or application form, including, if applicable, drug suppliers.  In general, UHN should not be indicated as a sponsor unless the UHN is a regulatory sponsor, then the entity that funds the study should be indicated as well as non-for-profit status of the UHN.

sections.heading: Informed Consent Form for Participation in a Research Study
sections.sub_section: Emergency Contact Number (24 hours / 7 days a week):
complexity: Not in protocol - requires manual entry
sections.instructions:
This may be a pager number (in this case change the heading to “Pager number”).  Clearly provide instructions for whom to contact, e.g. Principal Investigator, Co-investigator, a staff physician on call (e.g., “Ask for the Medical Oncologist on call”). The contact person must be knowledgeable about the study.

A research fellow can only be indicated as a contact person if discussed with and approved by the REB.

Should a pager or hospital locating be used, provide brief instructions on how to page/use locating (e.g. “Ask hospital locating to page the study doctor”)

Should communication via e-mail be suggested, when providing an e-mail address, include a disclaimer (as applicable). Only UHN email addresses should be provided to participants.

Note that non-emergency contact numbers are noted at the end of the consent form, under section heading "Contacts".
sections.suggested_text:
<If email communication is suggested>
Please note that communication via e-mail is not absolutely secure. Thus, please do not communicate personal sensitive information via e-mail.
OR
Please note that the security of e-mail messages is not guaranteed. Messages may be forged, forwarded, kept indefinitely, or seen by others using the Internet. Do not use e-mail to discuss information you think is sensitive. Do not use e-mail in an emergency since e-mail may be delayed.

<Note non-emergency contact information>
Non-Emergency contact numbers are noted at the end of this document under the section heading “Contacts”.

sections.heading: INTRODUCTION
complexity: Not in protocol - requires manual entry
sections.instructions:
STUDY TYPE: Determine whether this is a 'study' or a 'clinical trial' (a type of study that involves research). Use the protocol title and objectives section. Phase I–IV interventional studies are typically 'clinical trials'; observational or non-interventional studies are typically 'studies'.

{{main features of population}}: Explain the main features of the population to which the research applies. Do not include an extensive list of inclusion/exclusion criteria. A high-level summary is required (e.g., 'you have been diagnosed with [condition]', 'you are a healthy adult volunteer').

DECISION TIMEFRAME (choose exactly one — these are mutually exclusive):
  Option A (default): 'Please take your time in making your decision.' — use when no strict enrollment window applies.
  Option B: 'The study staff will tell you about the study timelines for making your decision.' — use ONLY when participants must start intervention within a specific timeframe due to best practices for the participant population/disease.

{{Specify other potential participant concerns}}: Specify any other areas where participants might be concerned about a potential penalty or discrimination (e.g., 'The decision will not affect your employment'). Omit if no such concerns apply.

{{impact on future healthcare options}}: If the study may affect future healthcare options, include the optional sentence beginning 'However; it may affect your future health care options…'. This typically applies to studies involving investigational therapies that may exclude participants from future treatment lines.

SUBSTITUTE DECISION MAKER (SDM): If consent may be sought through a substitute decision maker, prepend the SDM paragraph (see suggested_text) before the main introduction paragraph.
sections.required_text:
You are being invited to participate in a research {{study/clinical trial (a type of study that involves research)}}. You are invited to participate in this study because you {{main features of population}}. This consent form provides you with information to help you make an informed choice. Please read this document carefully and ask any questions you may have. All your questions should be answered to your satisfaction before you decide whether to participate in this research study. You may find it helpful to discuss it with your friends and family.

<If time permits — CHOOSE ONE of the following two options, do not include both>:
Please take your time in making your decision.
OR
<For clinical trials where participants must start intervention within a specific timeframe due to best practices for the participant population/disease>
The study staff will tell you about the study timelines for making your decision.

Taking part in this study is voluntary. You have the option to not participate at all or you may choose to leave the study at any time. Whatever you choose, it will not affect the usual medical care that you receive outside the study. <If the study may affect future healthcare options, include:> However; it may affect your future health care options. This will be discussed with you. If you have any questions about this, you can ask the study team. {{Specify other potential participant concerns, such as penalties, discrimination, or impact on employment — omit if none apply}}
sections.suggested_text:
<<If consent is sought through a Substitute Decision Maker — prepend this paragraph before the main introduction>>
As a Substitute Decision Maker, you are being asked to provide informed consent on behalf of a person who is not able to consent for themselves. Your decision should be based on what the person would want if they were able to make their own decision. If you are uncertain, consider what is in their best interest.

<<Standard introduction paragraph (always include)>>
[See required_text above]

sections.heading: IS THERE A CONFLICT OF INTEREST?
complexity: Not in protocol - requires manual entry
sections.instructions:
ALWAYS include this section — every ICF must declare conflicts of interest (or state there are none).

Describe any conflict of interest that exists or may appear to exist for any investigator, study staff member, or member of their immediate family. A conflict of interest exists if there is a potential personal benefit — including academic achievement, publication credit, or presentation of results. Common examples include: speaker's fees, travel assistance, consultant fees, honoraria, gifts, and intellectual property rights (patents).

A complete declaration must include:
1. The IDENTITY of the person with the conflict of interest
2. The TYPE of incentive or inducement
3. The SOURCE of the benefit

SELECT the most appropriate option from the suggested_text below:
- Option 1: Investigator receives personal financial payment from an external source
- Option 2: The institution/hospital receives financial payment (indirect conflict)
- Option 3: Sponsor reimburses hospital/researcher for study costs (sponsored studies — most common)
- Option 4: Researchers have a general interest in completing the study (internally funded studies — minimal conflict)

Note: This section is marked 'Not in protocol' because specific financial relationships between investigators and sponsors are typically not documented in the clinical protocol itself. The study team must provide this information directly.
sections.suggested_text:
The {{study doctor/researcher}}, {{insert name}}, is receiving personal financial payment from {{identify source of funds}} for {{include reason for payment}}. You may request details about this payment.
OR
The {{insert recipient of funding}} is receiving financial payment from the {{Sponsor/Funder}} to cover the cost of conducting this study.
OR
<For sponsored studies>
{{Insert name of company}}, the sponsor of this study, will reimburse the hospital and researcher for the costs of doing this study. All of these people have an interest in completing this study. Their interests should not influence your decision to participate in this study.
OR
<For internally funded studies>
Researchers have an interest in completing this study. Their interests should not influence your decision to participate in this study.

sections.heading: WHAT IS THE BACKGROUND INFORMATION FOR THIS STUDY?
complexity: Not in protocol - requires manual entry
sections.instructions:
Describe (in lay language) the background information relevant to the study, including (as applicable): the standard of care for the population, the reason for conducting the clinical trial, and the nature of the application with Health Canada.

HEALTH CANADA OVERSIGHT: Determine from the protocol whether the study involves a drug, device, or natural health product regulated by Health Canada (i.e., the study has an IND, IDE, or similar regulatory filing with Health Canada). If YES, include the required_text block below using the applicable option:
  - Option 1: Product IS approved in Canada but used OUTSIDE its approved parameters (new indication, different dose/schedule) → use Option 1 wording
  - Option 2: Product is NOT yet approved anywhere in Canada → use Option 2 wording
If the study does NOT involve Health Canada regulatory oversight (e.g., observational study, approved device used within approved parameters, investigator-initiated non-drug study), OMIT the required_text block entirely and proceed with suggested_text only.

AFTER the Health Canada statement (if applicable), always include the suggested_text content describing the condition's standard of care and background rationale for the study.
sections.required_text:
<<Include ONLY for studies under Health Canada oversight — choose exactly ONE of the two options below>>

<<Option 1: Product/agent/device IS approved in Canada but used outside approved parameters (e.g., approved for a different condition, or at a different dose/schedule)>>
{{Insert name(s) of product/agent/device}} is approved in Canada to treat {{specify condition/disease}}. Health Canada, the regulatory body that oversees the use of {{natural health products/drugs/devices}} in Canada, has not approved the sale or use of {{insert name(s) of product/agent/device}} for {{specify change from approved parameters}}. Health Canada has allowed {{insert name(s) of product/agent/device}} to be used in this study.

<<Option 2: Product/agent/device is NOT approved by Health Canada at all>>
Health Canada, the regulatory body that oversees the use of {{natural health products/drugs/devices}} in Canada, has not approved the sale or use of {{insert name(s) of product/agent/device}}. Health Canada has allowed {{insert name(s) of product/agent/device}} to be used in this study.
sections.suggested_text:
The standard or usual treatment for {{specify condition}} is {{describe the standard treatment}}.

{{Insert name(s) of product/agent/device}} is a new type of {{describe - natural health product/drug/device, etc.}} for {{specify condition}}. Laboratory tests show that it may {{explain laboratory results in lay terminology}}. It has been studied in a few people and seems promising but it is not clear if it can offer better results than standard treatment.

This is the first time this has been tested in people.

sections.heading: WHY IS THIS STUDY BEING DONE?
sections.instructions:
STEP 1 — IDENTIFY STUDY PHASE/TYPE: Look for the study phase or type explicitly stated in the protocol (e.g., 'Phase I', 'Phase II', 'Phase III', 'Phase IV', 'pilot study', 'feasibility study').

STEP 2 — SELECT ONE BLOCK AND OUTPUT IT AS THE ENTIRE SECTION CONTENT:
Each option in suggested_text already begins with 'The purpose of this study is to…' — it IS the required_text fulfilled for that phase. Choose exactly one block, fill in all {{placeholders}} with study-specific details from the protocol in plain lay language (Grade 6 reading level), and output ONLY that filled-in block. 
  • Pilot / Feasibility study → Option A
  • Phase I safety (standard design) → Option B
  • Phase I dose-escalation / dose-finding → Option C
  • Phase II (efficacy / effects) → Option D
  • Phase III non-placebo-controlled → Option E
  • Phase III placebo-controlled → Option F
  • Phase IV (post-approval) → Option G
  • No matching phase (e.g., registry, non-interventional, observational) → Write a single custom sentence: 'The purpose of this study is to {{study-specific description}}.' Do not append any phase block.

IF A PLACEBO IS USED: Append the placebo explanation sub-block from suggested_text (the '<If ANY placebo is used>' paragraph) after the chosen phase block, regardless of which option was selected.
sections.required_text: The purpose of this study is to {{explain the purpose of the study}}.
sections.suggested_text:
NOTE: The blocks below are MUTUALLY EXCLUSIVE. Choose exactly ONE block that matches the study phase/type. Do not include multiple phase blocks.

<<OPTION A — Pilot / Feasibility Study>>
The purpose of this study, called a pilot study or a feasibility study, is to test the study plan and to find out whether enough participants will join a larger study and accept the study procedures. This type of study involves a small number of participants and so it is not expected to prove safety or effectiveness of {{insert study intervention}}. The results may be used as a guide for larger studies, although there is no guarantee that they will be conducted. Participation in a pilot study does not mean that you will be eligible to participate in a future larger study.

<<OPTION B — Phase I Study (safety, standard design)>>
The purpose of this study is to test the safety of {{describe, e.g., natural health product/drug/device, insert name(s) of product/agent/device}}, to see what effects it has on individuals with {{specify condition}}.

<<OPTION C — Phase I Study (dose-escalation / dose-finding design — use instead of Option B if the protocol describes dose escalation)>>
The purpose of this study is to find the highest dose of {{describe, e.g., natural health product/drug/device, insert name(s) of product/agent/device}}, that can be given without causing very severe side effects. This is done by starting at a dose lower than the one that does not cause side effects in animals.

Participants are given {{insert name(s) of product/agent/device}} and are watched very closely to see what side effects they have and to make sure the side effects are not severe. If the side effects are not severe, then new participants will be given a higher dose of {{insert name(s) of product/agent/device}}. Participants joining this study later on will get higher doses of {{insert name(s) of product/agent/device}} than participants who join earlier.

<Include if dose-escalation continues to maximum tolerated dose:>
This will continue until a dose is found that causes severe but temporary side effects. Doses higher than that will not be given. Your study doctor will tell you at which dose level you will start the study drug.

<<OPTION D — Phase II Study>>
The purpose of this study is to find out what effects {{describe, e.g., natural health product/drug/device, insert name(s) of product/agent/device}}, has on individuals with {{specify condition}}.

<<OPTION E — Phase III Study (non-placebo-controlled comparative)>>
The purpose of this study is to compare the effects of {{describe, e.g., natural health product/drug/device, insert name(s) of product/agent/device}}, to other {{natural health products/drugs/devices}}, which are commonly-used for {{specify condition}}, on individuals with {{specify condition}}.

<<OPTION F — Phase III Placebo-Controlled Study>>
The purpose of this study is to find out {{specify purpose, e.g., whether it is better to receive insert name(s) of product/agent/device}}, or better to receive no additional intervention. To do this, some of the participants in this study will get {{insert name(s) of product/agent/device}} and others will receive a placebo (a substance that looks like the study {{natural health product/drug/device}} but does not have any active or medicinal ingredients).

<If ANY placebo is used — include this explanation regardless of phase:>
A placebo is given in this study to reduce the chances of believing that a disease is getting better because one is receiving a new drug. Really, we don't know if the new drug or no drug {{insert, if applicable, 'with or without the standard drug'}} is better than the other so we are doing this study to try and find that out.

<<OPTION G — Phase IV Study>>
The purpose of this study is to look at an approved intervention to obtain additional information about {{specify purpose e.g., benefits, side effects, etc.}}.

sections.heading: WHAT OTHER CHOICES ARE THERE?
sections.instructions:
Explain the alternative options applicable to the study population, and their important potential benefits and risks.

List applicable treatments available to participants. Should any of the study drug(s)/intervention(s) be available outside of participation in the study, they should be listed here.

Do not include: “This study has been approved by the research ethics committee….” since this may appear to offer a guarantee of safety.
sections.suggested_text:
<Suggestion for therapeutic intervention studies (modify as applicable if there is no other alternative treatment available)>
You do not have to take part in this study in order to receive treatment or care.  Other options (in addition to the standard or usual treatment described above) may include, but are not limited to:

<List applicable treatments available to participants (examples below may be used as applicable). Should any of the study drug(s)/intervention(s) be available outside of participation in the study, they should be listed here. >
• no therapy at this time
• palliative care or Best Supportive Care (BSC). This type of care helps reduce pain, tiredness, appetite problems and other problems. It does not treat your condition directly, but instead tries to improve how you feel. Best Supportive Care tries to keep you as active and comfortable as possible.
• other research studies may be available if you do not take part in this study

Please talk to your usual doctor or the study doctor about the known benefits and risks of these other options before you decide to take part in this study. Your usual doctor or the study doctor can also discuss with you what will happen if you decide not to undertake any treatment at this time.

<<Suggestion for studies using healthy volunteers>>
You do not have to take part in this study.

sections.heading: HOW MANY PEOPLE WILL TAKE PART IN THIS STUDY?
complexity: Not in protocol - requires manual entry
sections.instructions: Note: The length of individual participation in the study should be discussed below in the “HOW LONG WILL PARTICIPANTS BE IN THE STUDY?” section.
sections.required_text:
It is anticipated that about {{insert total number of study participants}} people will take part in this study. {{If research is multi-site, note the locations of other research sites and the number of participants from UHN}}.

This study should take {{total study length in months or years}} to complete and the results should be known in about {{time for anticipated analysis in months or years}}.

sections.heading: WHAT WILL HAPPEN DURING THIS STUDY?
sections.instructions: If there is more than one study group, describe how participants are placed into study groups.  Provide a lay description appropriate to the specific protocol.
sections.suggested_text:
<If there is more than one study group: example for randomized studies>

If you decide to participate then you will be "randomized" into one of the groups described below. Randomization means that you are put into a group by chance (like flipping a coin). There is no way to predict which group you will be assigned to. You will have {{explain probability of randomization e.g., an equal/one in three chance of being placed in either/any group}}.  Neither you, the study staff, nor the study doctors can choose what group you will be in.
- Participants in group 1: {{explain what will happen for this group with clear indication of which interventions depart from routine care}}
- Participants in group 2: {{explain what will happen for this group with clear indication of which interventions depart from routine care}}

sections.heading: WHAT WILL HAPPEN DURING THIS STUDY?
sections.instructions: Explain whether participants or others will know which group the participant will be in.
sections.suggested_text:
<For open label, randomized studies>
You will be told which group you are in.

<For single-blind studies>
You will not know which group you are in, but the study doctor and study staff will.

When you finish you participation in the study and would like to know what study group you were in, let the study team know. Please note that you may not be able to get this information until the entire study is completed.

<For double-blind studies>
This is a double-blind study, which means that neither you, the study doctors, the study staff, nor your usual health care providers will know which group you are in. Your group assignment can be identified if medically necessary.

When you finish you participation in the study and would like to know what study group you were in, let the study team know. Please note that you may not be able to get this information until the entire study is completed.

<Example for trials with intervention assigned based on protocol-specific criteria>
If you decide to participate then you will be assigned into one of the groups described below.  The group you are assigned to will be determined by {{specify assignment criteria e.g. the treatment you have previously received}}.  You will be told which group you are in.

<If applicable, include the following>

Once a certain number of participants have entered the intervention phase of the study from all of the research sites combined, no more participants will be enrolled into the study at any site.

sections.heading: WHAT WILL HAPPEN DURING THIS STUDY?
sections.instructions:
CONDITIONAL: Include this sub-section only when the study has clearly defined sequential periods (e.g., screening, run-in, treatment, follow-up). Omit for simple single-period studies.

List the study periods in the order participants will move through them. Common periods include: Screening Period, Run-in / Washout Period, Treatment / Intervention Period, End-of-Treatment Visit, Early Termination Visit, and Follow-up Period.

For each period, describe what happens to participants, the approximate duration, and any key procedures. Keep descriptions brief — detailed procedures are covered in section 12.

Extract the study periods and their durations directly from the protocol (typically in the 'Study Design' or 'Study Schedule' sections).
sections.suggested_text:
<Include this sub-section only if the study has multiple defined sequential periods>

Study Periods

Your participation in this study is divided into the following periods:

<Screening Period (include if applicable)>
Screening Period (approximately {{duration, e.g., 2–4 weeks}}): Before joining the study, you will be screened to confirm you are eligible to participate. This involves {{brief description of screening procedures, e.g., blood tests, physical exam, review of medical history}}.

<Run-in / Washout Period (include if applicable)>
Run-in / Washout Period (approximately {{duration}}): Before the study begins, you will be asked to stop taking {{specify medications/substances}} for approximately {{duration}} to allow them to leave your system.

<Treatment / Intervention Period (always include if there is a defined treatment period)>
Treatment Period (approximately {{duration}}): You will receive {{describe intervention}}. This is the main part of the study.

<End-of-Treatment Visit (include if specified in protocol)>
End-of-Treatment Visit: At the end of the treatment period, you will attend a visit at {{location}} for {{brief description of procedures}}.

<Early Termination Visit (include if specified in protocol)>
Early Termination Visit: If you or the study doctor decide you should stop the study early, you will attend a final visit within {{timeframe}} of stopping.

<Follow-up Period (include if specified in protocol)>
Follow-up Period (approximately {{duration}}): After you finish the treatment, we will continue to follow your health for approximately {{duration}}. Follow-up will consist of {{describe method, e.g., clinic visits every X weeks, phone calls}}.

sections.heading: WHAT IS THE STUDY INTERVENTION?
sections.instructions: Describe intervention by study group, including a clear identification of experimental components of the study. Provide a detailed description appropriate to the specific protocol. Ensure that the Group/Arm names and descriptions are consistent with the protocol for multi-group studies.
sections.suggested_text:
<<Suggestion for single arm studies>>
Experimental Intervention:
If you agree to take part in this study, you will {{identify intervention, including description of method}}. The procedure will take about {{length of time}}. This will happen every {{frequency}} weeks for {{duration}} months.

<<Suggestion for multi-group studies. Ensure that the Group/Arm names and descriptions are consistent with the protocol.>>
• Group 1 (Experimental intervention): Standard intervention {{specify drug name/regimen/ intervention}} plus experimental intervention {{specify drug name/regimen/intervention}}.
If you are randomized to this group you will {{identify intervention, including description of method}}. The procedure will take about {{length of time}}. This will happen every {{frequency}} weeks for {{duration}} months.

• Group 2 (Non-Experimental Intervention): Standard intervention {{specify drug name/regimen /intervention}}.
If you are randomized to this group you will {{identify intervention, including description of method}}. The procedure will take about {{length of time}}. This will happen every {{frequency}} weeks for {{duration}} months.

sections.heading: WHAT ELSE DO I NEED TO KNOW ABOUT THE STUDY INTERVENTION?
sections.instructions:
Include information about what may happen if side effects are encountered (changes to intervention, etc).

If participation in the study restricts future treatment options, inform participants of details.

If standard treatment is being withheld or withdrawn, inform participants of details.

For studies with washout period, provide details on washout requirements
sections.suggested_text:
<Include the relevant information from the selection below.>

If you have side effects while you are on this study, the study doctor may make changes {{specify}}.

<If participation in the study restricts future treatment options, inform participants of details>
If you are in {{identify restriction}}, you may not be able to receive {{identify any future treatment options that participant would be excluded from in the future}}.

<If standard treatment is being withheld or withdrawn>
Normally, you would receive {{identify standard treatment}} for {{specify condition}}.  If you decide to take part in this study, you {{will/may}} not receive this usual treatment.

<For studies with washout period, provide details on washout requirements>
As part of this study, you will be asked to stop taking {{identify washout agent}} for a period of {{insert washout period in weeks/months}} before you begin the study intervention.

sections.heading: WHAT ARE THE STUDY PROCEDURES?
sections.instructions:
Describe the procedures that are used in the study that are non-experimental.

The consent should focus on the research related procedures and discuss standard of care where necessary. Each procedure/intervention should be described only once.

Quantify the amount of body fluids/tissue to be taken at each visit. If quantifying in spoons, indicate in mL as well.

If just a few visits are required, indicate here the approximate duration of each visit. If applicable, provide details on hospitalizations and indicate if procedures are to be performed outside of UHN.  Should study procedures take place at varying UHN sites, indicate so.

If the participant’s medical records must be accessed for the purposes of the study, outline specific information that will be retrieved from the participant’s medical records.
sections.suggested_text:
<If just a few visits are required, indicate here the approximate duration of each visit.>
There will be {{number}} study visits during your participation in this study. Visit 1  will be {{duration}} and visit 2 will be {{duration}}.

<If applicable, provide details on hospitalizations and indicate if procedures are to be performed outside of UHN.  Should study procedures take place at varying UHN sites, indicate so.>
Study visit 1 will take place at {{location}} and Study visit 2 will take place at {{location}}.

sections.heading: WHAT ARE THE STUDY PROCEDURES?
sections.instructions:
List (in bullet format) any standard procedures (e.g. MRI, blood draws, etc.) and tests that are outside of standard of care. Include a lay explanation, if not obvious, of what each test involves.

Describe the method of blood/urine/other sample collection and associated risks.  Specify number of samples to be collected and timing. If blood is collected, specify the amount and types of tests to be done on the samples. If blood is collected for the purpose of HIV or Hepatitis B/C tests, include a statement notifying that "positive test results will be reported to local health authorities/Public Health". Specify the location (city and country only) and purpose for the review.
sections.suggested_text: See attached for suggested list of procedures and descriptions.

sections.heading: WHAT ARE THE STUDY PROCEDURES?
sections.instructions:
If there are experimental procedures or medical tests, include this section.  Any standard procedures (e.g., MRI, blood draw, etc.) that are outside of standard of care should be included in the ‘non-experimental procedures’ section – this section is for procedures that are experimental (e.g., being tested as part of the research).

Explain any risks of experimental procedures and medical tests in the RISK section.

List the procedures and tests. Include explanation of what each test involves and the purpose/reason/rationale for including it in the research.
sections.suggested_text:
The following tests are considered experimental and will only be done for participants on this study:
{{List experimental procedures and tests. Include explanation of what each test involves and the purpose/reason/rationale for including it in the research.}}

sections.heading: WHAT ARE THE STUDY PROCEDURES?
sections.instructions: Specify how many focus group meetings and information about timing. Specify the length in minutes or hours and the location of the meeting. Explain the topics of discussion. Specify if any recording devices will be used.
sections.suggested_text:
You will be asked to attend {{specify how many focus group(s) and provide information about timing}}.  A focus group is a small group of representative people who are asked to speak about their opinions as part of the research.  A moderator will organize the focus group(s). sections.instructions:
If the study includes genetic testing, include a statement noting the risk of discovering inheritable conditions or actionable genetic incidental findings during the course of the research. In general, these risks are not applicable to pharmacogenetic or pharmacogenomic testing of specific drugs or drug combinations.

If there will be sequencing of a human germline or somatic specimen with the intent to generate the genome or exome sequence of that specimen, include the statement about whole genome sequencing.

If applicable to target population (First Nations, Inuit, or Métis persons), also include the following statement: "If you are a First Nations, Inuit, or Métis person who has contact with Elders, you may want to talk to them before you make a decision about this research study. Elders may have concerns about some research procedures including genetic testing.
sections.suggested_text:
This study involves genetic testing.  Researchers will be looking at your genes (DNA).
Hereditary genetic testing (to look at whether {{specify condition}} runs in families) {{will not/will/may be}} done on these samples.

<Include if applicable (i.e., sequencing of a human germline or somatic specimen with the intent to generate the genome or exome sequence of that specimen):>
The study {{will/may}} involve whole genome sequencing. Whole genome sequencing is the analysis of the complete set of genetic instructions in a cell.

Every person has their own unique set of genes or “genome”. The reason this is important is because genetic results might contain information (for example, an inherited genetic disease) that could impact you or your biological (blood) relatives.  When you donate your genetic information or materials you are sharing information about yourself, and it can be used to identify these relatives.

Even with protections in place, absolute confidentiality cannot be guaranteed. Advances in technology could also increase the risk that your genetic samples and results could be linked back to you or your relatives.  There is no way to predict what effects such an information loss would have. For example, if an insurer, a current or future employer, or law enforcement were to learn your genetic code it could result in loss of privacy and to possible future discrimination in employment or insurance against you or your relatives.  Even though this risk is unlikely, we think you should be aware. However, the only identifying information that will be present on the sample is your study number.
Due to the rapid pace of technological advances, the potential future use of genetic information is unknown and therefore the potential future risks also are unknown and there may be a risk that the genetic information in the samples could be linked back to you.

You will be {{given the choice/not given the choice}} to find out about genetics testing results.

<<If applicable to target population>> If you are a First Nations, Inuit, or Métis person who has contact with Elders, you may want to talk to them before you make a decision about this research study. Elders may have concerns about some research procedures including genetic testing.

sections.heading: CAN PARTICIPANTS CHOOSE TO LEAVE THE STUDY?
sections.instructions:
OUTPUT STRUCTURE: Your filled_template must contain the required_text verbatim as the opening paragraph, followed by the applicable suggested_text blocks resolved for this study.

For the suggested_text, resolve each conditional as follows:
- <For clinical trials with regulatory oversight>: Include if the study is a regulated clinical trial (Phase I–IV interventional study with Health Canada or FDA oversight).
- <If the participant can withdraw information collected prior to withdrawal>: Include if the protocol allows data withdrawal — this is the more permissive option; use it unless the protocol explicitly states data cannot be withdrawn.
- <Studies involving tissue/blood/body fluids>: Include if the study collects biological samples.

Do NOT include the line 'You may be asked questions...' for non-interventional studies where no such exit procedures exist.

IMPORTANT: Never imply written withdrawal is required. Do not include language suggesting the sponsor should be contacted directly by the participant.
sections.required_text: You can choose to end your participation in this research (called withdrawal) at any time without having to provide a reason.  If you choose to withdraw from the study, you are encouraged to contact the research team.

You may be asked questions about your experience with the study intervention and to have laboratory tests and physical examinations considered necessary to safely stop your study involvement.

<For clinical trials with regulatory oversight>
If you leave the study, information that was recorded before you withdrew will still be used by the researchers in order to answer the research question, but no information will be collected without your permission after you withdraw from the study.

<If the participant can withdraw information collected prior to withdrawal> If you decide to leave the study, you can ask that the information that was collected about you not be used for the study.  Let the study doctor know if you choose this. Otherwise, information that was recorded before you withdrew will still be used by the researchers for the purposes of the study, but no information will be collected after you withdraw from the study.

<Studies involving tissue/blood/body fluids>
If you decide to leave the study, you have the right to request withdrawal of your {{insert types of samples as applicable to study – e.g. blood, tissue, etc}}. Let your study doctor know. Otherwise, samples collected before you withdrew will still be used by the researchers for the purposes of the study, but no information will be collected after you withdraw from the study. The sponsor will keep and use any research results obtained prior to your withdrawal of consent.

sections.heading: WHAT ARE THE RISKS OR HARMS OF PARTICIPATING IN THIS STUDY?
sections.instructions:
YOUR SCOPE FOR THIS SECTION IS NARROW — read carefully.

Your filled_template must contain exactly TWO things and nothing else:
  1. The required_text verbatim (the two opening sentences — do NOT change a word).
  2. The applicable suggested_text with placeholders filled and conditionals resolved:
       - Fill {{insert name of product/agent/device}} with the study intervention name from the protocol.
       - Include the <For studies using non-marketed drugs...> paragraph ONLY if the study uses a non-marketed or investigational drug/device. Omit it for approved standard-of-care drugs.
       - Always include the 911 / emergency room sentence.

DO NOT extract or include any list of side effects or risks here. The detailed risk content belongs exclusively in sub-sections 18.1 (intervention risks) and 18.2 (procedure risks), which are processed separately. Including risk lists here will cause duplication in the final ICF.
sections.required_text:
You may experience side effects from participating in this study. Some side effects are known and are listed below, but there may be other side effects that are not expected. You should discuss these with the study doctor.

The study doctor will watch you closely to see if you have side effects.
sections.suggested_text:
When possible, other medicine will be given to you to make side effects less serious and more tolerable. Many side effects go away shortly after the study intervention is stopped, but in some cases side effects can be serious, long-lasting, permanent, or may even cause death.

<For studies using non-marketed drugs or other investigational interventions, if applicable. >
If you experience a side effect that requires treatment between regular clinic/hospital visits, it is important that you make every effort to return to the clinic/hospital where {{insert name of product/agent/device}} was given. Because {{insert name of product/agent/device}} is experimental and is only used in clinics/hospitals involved in research studies, any side effects may be best treated by these clinics/hospitals.

If you experience a serious side effect and need immediate treatment and are unable to return to the clinic/hospital, please call 911 or go to the nearest emergency room. Then the study doctor should be contacted as soon as possible.

sections.heading: WHAT ARE THE RISKS OR HARMS OF PARTICIPATING IN THIS STUDY?
sections.instructions:
Nature of risks to include:  Describe all reasonably foreseeable risks, harms, or discomforts.  Include both, physical and psychological/emotional, risks as applicable to the research. A rare but serious finding from previous studies (e.g., death, severe pneumonitis, acute renal failure etc.) should be placed at the top of the list of side effects or should be bolded.

Language:  Include lay language explanation of any side effects

Categorization:  When detailed information about the side effect profile for the intervention is known, categorize risks by frequency.  Examples of these categories are provided below - other categorizations may be used depending on the presentation of risks in the Investigator Brochure/Product Monograph.

Information to provide:  address frequency, severity, and long term impact or reversibility.  When applicable, specific symptoms for serious side effects of which the participant should be aware (e.g., in order to seek immediate medical assistance) should be included.

It is not necessary to describe the risks associated with tests or procedures with which the participant population would already be familiar

Explain the significance of a side effect if not obvious

Include risk of receiving placebo/delayed surgery/delayed/withdrawn standard of care treatment, if applicable

Address psychological risks such as anxiety, distress, embarrassment, or feelings of sadness that may arise from questionnaires and interviews about sensitive issues (e.g. mental health, sexuality)

Separate side effects of each drug and procedure as appropriate

Consider using bullets (or sub-sections, as appropriate) for each of the side effects

List each side effect of a drug/procedure only once, provide a full description of the side effect under the same bullet, and indicate its severity, if applicable.

For Phase II and Phase III studies where previous safety data are available, the REB expects that the upper range of frequency be provided. A “greater than 10/20%” response is generally not acceptable without justification. It should be noted that the REB evaluates the presentation of the side effects on “study-by-study” basis, severity of the side effects, and their relevance to the study population.  Serious side effects that have not been clearly linked to the study drug should also be included.

Possible treatment options of side effects should not be discussed in the consent form as, should a side effect occur, it would be treated according to the individual’s situation and might differ from the options listed in the consent form. Moreover, it may leave the impression that treatment options of side effects are part of the study intervention, while they are not.

Should an extensive list of side effects per drug be identified, to improve readability and to increase the participant’s comprehension of the information, consider grouping side effects in a meaningful way for participants, e.g. per system, per syndrome etc.

Presenting an extensive list of side effects in an alphabetical order is not acceptable.

Should a standard of care drug(s)/treatment be administered as part of the study intervention, most common and most severe side effects from that standard of care intervention should be presented.

Should an experimental combination of the drugs be administered during the study, potential side effects should be listed for each of the drugs individually. Then, under the combination, only side effects not listed for either of the drugs and/or side effects that have been seen at an increased frequency than either of the drugs administered alone should be presented. After side effects per drug are presented, the consent form should have some language indicating that “any of these side effect may occur with the combination of the drugs, some of them could be more severe, may occur in an increased frequency and new side effects, not seen with either of the drugs, may happen.”
If a combination has been studied extensively then, e.g. at the sponsor’s request to have all side effects listed for the combination, in addition to the above, an appendix to the consent form presenting a comprehensive list of the side effects of the combination could be considered.
If more data on a combination than on the study drugs individually are available. Then the side effects could be listed for the combination first followed by the presentation of the side effects for each drug individually presenting only side effects not listed for the combination and/or side effects that have been seen at the increased frequency with either of the drugs administered alone.

Risks and side effects related to the experimental intervention {{insert name of product/agent/device}} we are studying include:
{{see instructions for what to list}}

<If the study drug is used in combination with standard treatment, the consent should include the following>
You will receive the standard treatment for the condition you have.  An experimental {{specify intervention}} is being added to this.  This combination may change the side effects or the effectiveness of the standard treatment.  This may mean that you experience more side effects than you would with the standard treatment alone.  It may also mean that the standard treatment does not work as expected.

<If participation in this study puts the participants at increased risk of long-term effects such as cancer, include the following>
