sections.heading: WHAT ARE THE RISKS OR HARMS OF PARTICIPATING IN THIS STUDY?
sections.instructions:
CONDITIONAL: Include this sub-section only when the study involves specific procedures whose risks need to be described (e.g., imaging, blood draws, biopsies). Omit if all procedures are routine and already familiar to the study population.

STEP 1 — Identify the study's non-standard procedures by searching the protocol directly (look in the 'Study Procedures', 'Assessments', or 'Schedule of Events' sections). Build a list of every procedure type mentioned (e.g., MRI, CT scan, blood draw, biopsy, PET scan, ECG, questionnaires, etc.).

STEP 2 — From the suggested_text below, keep ONLY the risk entries that match the procedures you identified. Remove every risk entry for procedures NOT present in this study. Do not fabricate risk descriptions — use only the entries provided.

STEP 3 — For any kept risk entry that contains {{placeholders}}, fill them in using study-specific details from the protocol (e.g., number of scans, quantified radiation dose). If a placeholder cannot be filled from the protocol, write [TO BE FILLED MANUALLY].
sections.suggested_text:
Risks of X-ray - Exposes you to doses of radiation. {{Indicate risks.}}

Risks of MRI - It is painless but noisy. If you have any metal in your body, for your safety, please tell the staff.  There is no radiation. Some people may feel a little ‘closed-in’ the MRI machine, but you will be able to speak with someone at all times and can stop the test at any time.

Risks of MRI with Gadolinium Contrast - It is painless but noisy. If you have any metal in your body, for your safety, please tell the staff.  There is no radiation. Some people may feel a ‘closed-in’ the MR machine, but you will be able to speak with someone at all times and can stop the test at any time. Gadolinium is a contrast agent used in MRI imaging that helps to better see any abnormal tissues and see blood vessels. Gadolinium contrast is not safe to use in people who have kidney problems. If you have had a problem with your kidneys, please inform the study team of this problem before the procedure. There is a small risk {{quantify}} of allergic reaction with gadolinium that can include symptoms of itchiness or rash. Rarely, a serious reaction may develop involving your kidneys leading to hardened skin lesions all over your body and stiffening of your joints. You must seek medical attention immediately if you develop any symptoms of allergic reaction.

Risks of ECG - This test is painless, and you should not feel anything while the ECG is done.  Since the ECG machine will be connected to your body by sticky patches, like bandages, these may cause your skin to become mildly irritated.

Risks of MUGA Scan - Exposes you to low levels of radiation with the radioactive tracer. The radioactive tracer is eliminated in approximately 24 hours.

Risks of CT Scan - Exposes you to moderate levels of radiation.  One CT scan equals 70 chest x-rays.

Risks of CT Scan with Contrast - Exposes you to moderate levels of radiation.  One CT scan equals 70 chest x-rays.  You may develop an allergic reaction to the dye used for the CT scan.

Risks of a PET-CT Scan: PET-CT scans are painless but may make you feel uncomfortable. You may have an allergic reaction to the contrast agent/tracer. Please inform your study doctor if you have any history of allergic reactions to contrast agents or shellfish. Another potential risk is radiation exposure. We are exposed to approximately 1.6-1.8 mSv of background radiation per year in Canada. A single PET-CT has an average exposure of 25mSv. Since you will have {{indicate # of scans}} scans for this study, your radiation exposure will be about {{indicate #}} mSv over the course of the study. This is equivalent to approximately {{indicate # of years, e.g. 73.3 to 82.5}} years of natural background radiation. If you have concerns about the radiation you may get from a CT scan, PET scan, or any other imaging test that uses radiation, talk to your study doctor.

Risks of a Pulmonary Function Test - You may feel short of breath and light-headed while doing the test.

Risks of Dexa Scan - Exposes you to low levels of radiation, approximately 1/5 to 1/2 (20-50%) that of a chest x-ray.

Risks of Blood Transfusion - Even though you will be given blood that matches your blood type, it is still possible that you may have an allergic reaction to parts of the transfused blood.  All of our blood for transfusion comes from the {{insert, e.g., Canadian Blood Services}}. They have taken great care to make sure the blood you are given is safe.  However, there is a very small risk of getting an infectious disease from a blood transfusion.  It is also possible that a new disease could be found in donor’s blood in the future.

Risks of Blood Draw/Catheter Blood Draw - There is a possibility of pain, bruising, swelling, or infection related to giving blood.

Risks of Skin Photography - {{Insert, if applicable, risk of breach of confidentiality.}}

Risks of Videography - {{Insert, if applicable, risk of breach of confidentiality.}}

Risks of Questionnaires – {{Outline, if applicable, sensitivity of questions.}}

Risks of Tissue Sampling - {{Risk depends on the method (e.g. aspirate, punch biopsy, incision biopsy etc.), therefore, list applicable risks.}} This procedure has risks such as {{specify risks, e.g., blood loss, pain and rarely an infection at the biopsy site}}.

Risks of Archival Tumor Tissue Collection - There are no physical risks to you in releasing archived tumor samples for the purpose of this study since the tissue has already been obtained by a previous biopsy/surgical procedure. However, it is possible that, if your tumor tissue is released for this study, there will be insufficient tumor tissue for any possible future testing that requires tumor tissue. Please speak to the study doctor to discuss this possibility.

Focus Group/Interview - You may become uncomfortable while discussing your experiences.  You may refuse to answer questions or leave the group/interview at any time if you experience any discomfort.

Other Concerns:
<Depending on study design and study population there might be other concerns/risks, e.g. risks from radiation, risks to fertility etc., that vary from individual to individual and, thus, are not reflected above. The following paragraph presents an example how other concerns could be reflected in the consent form. Should it be included, it shall be adjusted appropriately to the concerns, study design, and study population. >

If you have any questions or concerns about risks from {{insert study intervention; e.g. the radiation dose? you may receive from study procedures; whether your fertility (ability to have children) could be affected by the study intervention; whether your future treatment options could be affected by the study intervention}}, please discuss these questions and concerns with your study doctor.

sections.heading: WHAT ARE THE REPRODUCTIVE RISKS?
complexity: Not in protocol - requires manual entry
sections.instructions:
If the agent(s) used in the study present a real or potential risk of fetal or reproductive harm, this must be described.  Generic wording for unknown risk is included in template.

Some sponsors would like to follow pregnancy for safety reporting.  A person should not be asked to give consent to collect information about a future uncertain event at the time of consenting for the study since the circumstances surrounding the decisions could be different at that time. This is a decision that they and, possibly, their partner would want to make at that time.  Also, once they become pregnant, they are withdrawn from the study and all rights to collect information are lost. Therefore, pregnant participants must be consented to be followed. They should be re-consented using a separate consent form to give permission for their pregnancy to be followed.
sections.suggested_text:
The effects that {{insert name of product/agent/device}} may have on an unborn baby (fetus), eggs,  or sperm are unknown. You must not become pregnant, make your partner pregnant or donate eggs/sperm {{specify period e.g.}}, while taking {{insert name of product/agent/device}} and for {{identify post-intervention period}} after the last dose.  The study doctor will discuss family planning with you/your partner to ensure that {{you/they}} do not become pregnant or make your partner pregnant during the study.

<If there are known interactions or contraindications with specific methods, they should be included.>

<If there is a risk of sperm mutation or teratogenic risk, include the following:>
You should discuss these risks with sexual partner of the opposite sex.

The intervention used in this study may make you unable to have children in the future. The study doctor will discuss this with you.

You must not nurse (breastfeed) a baby {{specify period e.g.}}, while taking {{insert name of product/agent/device}} and for {{identify post-intervention period}} after the last dose because the drugs used in this study might be present in breast milk and could be harmful to a baby.

<For trials with pregnancy reporting, include the following>
If you become pregnant or make your partner pregnant {{specify period e.g}}., while taking {{insert name of product/agent/device}} and for {{identify post-intervention period}} after the last dose, you should immediately notify the study doctor.

The study doctor will ask if you/your partner are willing to provide information about the pregnancy as part of this study. You/ your partner will be given a separate consent form to sign to give permission for the collection of information on the pregnancy.   You or your partner may choose not to give consent for the collection of this information or may withdraw consent at any time without giving a reason.

If your pregnant partner does not provide permission for the pregnancy follow up, it will not impact your participation on the study and will not result in any penalty or affect your or your partner’s current or future health care.

sections.heading: WHAT ARE THE BENEFITS OF PARTICIPATING IN THIS STUDY?
complexity: Not in protocol - requires manual entry
sections.instructions: Inform participants of potential benefits to themselves and in general that may arise.  If there is no known benefit, ensure this is stated.
sections.suggested_text:
<If there is no likely medical benefit to participation (e.g., phase I study), include the following>
There are no medical benefits to you for taking part in this study.

<If the benefit is known, include>
The expected benefit from taking part in this study is {{specify}}.

<If the potential benefit is unknown, include>
If you agree to take part in this study, the experimental intervention may or may not be of direct benefit to you.

<For placebo controlled studies, include the following>
You will not benefit from the placebo used in this study.

<If applicable, include>
We hope the information learned from this study will help other people with {{specify condition}} in the future.

sections.heading: HOW WILL PARTICIPANT INFORMATION BE KEPT CONFIDENTIAL?
complexity: Not in protocol - requires manual entry
sections.instructions:
Note: Should there be disclosure of personal identifiers, i.e., disclosed on any research-related information/documents including samples or scans, or as part of the unique identifier, these disclosures must be justified in the REB application and accepted by the REB.

If other organizations have permission to receive study data, indicate this (in addition to the list for direct access), and include a brief description of their role in the research.

If race/ethnicity information is collected as part of the study, identify this and provide a rationale.

If the study involves a focus group/interview, include a statement on confidentiality measures.

If it involves a video/audio recording, describe confidentiality measures (who will have access, how long, where recordings will be retained).

If identifiable data are to be sent outside the institution, or the study uses a third party to perform study related activities, indicate who will be involved, for what purpose, and what identifyable data would be included.

If the study is a Health Canada regulated study, include a statement about the retention of PHI for 15 years. If the study is NOT Health Canada regulated, retention period is 10 years.
sections.required_text:
If you decide to participate in this study, the research team will only collect the information they need for this study.

Your data will be shared as described in this consent form and/or as required by law and/or applicable research regulations. Records identifying you at this Centre will be kept confidential and, to the extent permitted by applicable laws, will not be disclosed or made publicly available.

Authorized representatives of the following organizations may come to the hospital or be given remote access to an electronic portal (via Internet) to look at your original (identifiable) medical/clinical study records at the site where these records are held, to check that the information collected for the study is correct and follows proper laws and guidelines. When using the electronic portal, we will share your medical record number using a secure method, so that your records are included as part of their review.
• Representatives of the University Health network, including the UHN Research Ethics Board who oversees the ethical conduct of this study at UHN
• {{insert organizations requiring permission for direct access to participant medical records containing identifying information, and include a brief description of their role in the research. Example: Health Canada because they oversee the use of natural health products/drugs/devices in Canada}}

These individuals have completed privacy training and signed confidentiality agreements and/or are required by law to keep your information confidential.

Whether on-site or remotely, UHN makes all efforts to ensure that your information is shared in a way that is secure and private (encrypted). However, any electronic communication carries some risk of third parties gaining unauthorized access to information.

Information that is collected about you for the study (called study data) may also be sent to the organizations listed above. Any information about you that is sent out of the hospital will have a number and will not show any information that directly identifies you. The records received by these organizations will contain your  {{disclose identifiers, e.g. participant code, sex and year of birth}}.

If the results of this study are published, your identity will remain confidential. It is expected that the information collected during this study will be {{include description of proposed uses of data e.g. scientific community presentations, submissions to regulatory authorities}}.

Even though the likelihood that someone may identify you from the study data is very small, it can never be completely eliminated.

The study doctor will keep any personal health information about you in a secure and confidential location for {{if study is Health Canada regulated, indicate 15 years; otherwise, 10 years}} as required.

Your participation in this study will also be recorded in your medical record at this hospital.
sections.suggested_text:
<<If organizations have permission to receive study data (NOT direct access), include a brief description of their role in the research>>
The following organizations may also receive study data:

<<If race/ethnicity information is collected as part of the study>>
Studies involving humans sometimes collect information on race and ethnicity as well as other characteristics of individuals because these characteristics may influence how people respond to different interventions. Providing information on your race or ethnic origin is {{voluntary/required}}.

<<If an autopsy report is being provided to the sponsor, include the following>>
This study does not require that an autopsy be performed.  However, if an autopsy is performed for other reasons, and a copy of the report is provided to the study doctor, this report will be sent to the study sponsor as part of the study data collected for this study.  This report may contain other health information that is not required for study purposes.

<<If health information is being collected for other research/database>>
In addition to the data that will be collected for this study, the researchers will also be collecting the following personal health information: {{List all additional information being collected}}

These additional data are being collected to {{insert purpose}}. This additional information is not required for the purpose of this study, but for other research interests at {{insert organization name}}.

sections.heading: HOW WILL PARTICIPANT INFORMATION BE KEPT CONFIDENTIAL?
sections.sub_section: Research Information in Shared Clinical Records
sections.instructions:
OUTPUT STRUCTURE: Your filled_template must contain the required_text verbatim as the opening paragraph (the UHN hospital records / computer system statement — do not change a word), followed by any applicable suggested_text blocks.

For the suggested_text, resolve each conditional by searching the protocol:
- <<If data or samples are to be sent outside of Canada>>: Include if the protocol specifies data or samples will be transferred to sites outside Canada (e.g., to a central lab, sponsor, or database in the US or Europe).
- <<For studies using smartphones, apps or applicable technology>>: Include if the study uses a mobile app, wearable device, or third-party digital platform to collect study data. Fill in the app/tool/device name from the protocol.

If neither condition applies, output only the required_text.
sections.required_text: If you participate in this study, information about you from this research project may be stored in your hospital file and in the UHN computer system.  The UHN shares the patient information stored on its computers with other hospitals and health care providers in Ontario so they can access the information if it is needed for your clinical care.  The study team can tell you what information about you will be stored electronically and may be shared outside of the UHN.  If you have any concerns about this, or have any questions, please contact the UHN Privacy Office at 416-340-4800, x6937 (or by email at privacy@uhn.ca).
sections.suggested_text:
<<If data or samples are to be sent outside of Canada>>
Any information {{and/or samples}} sent outside of Canadian borders may increase the risk of disclosure of information because the laws in other countries dealing with protection of information may not be as strict as in Canada. However, all study data {{and/or samples}} that are transferred outside of Canada will be coded. This means they will not contain your personal identifying information such as your name, address, medical health number or contact information.

<<For studies using smartphones, apps or applicable technology to collect the study data>>
Data collected using the {{insert app/tool/device name}} reside on the {{insert name}} and no assurance can be made about their confidentiality or that they will only be used for research purposes.

sections.heading: HOW WILL PARTICIPANT INFORMATION BE KEPT CONFIDENTIAL?
sections.instructions:
CONDITIONAL: Include this sub-section only when the protocol specifies how participant data or samples will (or will not) be used for future research beyond the current study.

Determine from the protocol whether:
  a) De-identified data or samples are MANDATORY for future research sharing → include the 'coded data/samples for future research' block
  b) The study is US federally funded AND data/samples will NOT be used for future research → include the 'data will not be shared for future research' alternative block
  c) Focus groups or interviews are used → include the focus group confidentiality statement
  d) Video/audio recordings are made → include the recording retention and access statement

Omit this sub-section entirely if none of the above conditions apply.
sections.suggested_text:
<<If it is mandatory for de-identified data or samples be used or shared for future research, include>>
Your coded {{study data and/or coded samples}} may be used or shared with other researchers (inside and outside of Canada) for future studies.  “Coded” means that directly identifying information (such as your name and date of birth) will be replaced by a number, which will be applied to the {{study data and/or samples}}. The code matching your {{study data and/or samples}} with your name and other directly identifying study data will be kept by the UHN study team.
This may include storing the {{coded study data and/or samples}} in
- Controlled-access {{databases/biobanks}}.  Access to them is limited to researcher(s) who submit a study plan and who sign an agreement to use the coded study data and/or coded samples only for that research.
- Open access, publicly accessible databases.  Only very limited coded study data may be placed in such databases. The goal of sharing is to make more research possible.

You will not be asked if you agree to take part in future research studies using your {{study data and/or samples}}. You or your study doctor will not be told what type of research will be done. You will not be given reports or other information about any research that is done with your {{study data and/or samples}}.

<<If focus group/interview>>:
During the discussions, participants will be encouraged to refrain from using names.  If names or other identifying information is shared during the discussion, it will not be included in the written records.

<<If video/audio recording, describe confidentiality measures such as access controls and retention period>>
The video/audio recordings will be stored in a secure location and viewed only by members of the research team.  The recordings will be kept until they have been transcribed (turned into written records), and then they will be destroyed.

sections.heading: WILL FAMILY DOCTORS/HEALTH CARE PROVIDERS KNOW WHO IS PARTICIPATING IN THIS STUDY?
complexity: Not in protocol - requires manual entry
sections.instructions:
CHOOSE ONE of the two options in the suggested_text (they are mutually exclusive):
  - Option 1 (default for most therapeutic studies): Family doctor WILL or MAY be informed — use 'will' if it is standard practice to inform the family doctor; use 'may' if there are circumstances where the family doctor would not be informed.
  - Option 2: Family doctor will NOT be informed — use for studies where confidentiality considerations mean the family doctor should not be routinely notified (e.g., some psychiatric, sexual health, or healthy volunteer studies).

The applicable option is typically determined by the study team's standard practice for the relevant therapeutic area, not directly from the protocol.
sections.suggested_text:
<<Option 1 — Family doctor will or may be informed (most common)>>
Your family doctor/health care provider {{will/may}} be informed that you are taking part in a study so that you can be provided with appropriate medical care. If you do not want your family doctor/health care provider to be informed, please discuss this with the study team.

<<Option 2 — Family doctor will NOT be informed>>
OR
Your family doctor/health care provider will not be informed by the study team that you are taking part in the study. You can choose to let your family doctor/health care provider know, if you like.

sections.heading: WILL INFORMATION ABOUT THIS STUDY BE AVAILABLE?
sections.instructions:
This section has two components. Your filled_template must be assembled as follows:

1. CLINICAL TRIALS REGISTRY (required_text — CONDITIONAL): Determine from the protocol whether this is an interventional clinical trial (Phase I–IV, device trial, or similar study requiring regulatory registration). If YES, include the required_text paragraph verbatim — do NOT modify a single word. If NO (e.g., observational study, survey, retrospective chart review), omit the required_text entirely.

2. STUDY RESULTS SHARING (suggested_text — always include): Always append the suggested_text paragraph as-is after the required_text (if included). This text is standard boilerplate and requires no protocol extraction.
sections.required_text:
<Include ONLY for clinical trials — Do NOT modify this text>
A description of this clinical trial will be available on http://www.clinicaltrials.gov, as required by U.S. Law. This website will not include information that can identify you. At most, the website will include a summary of the results. You can search this website at any time.
sections.suggested_text: Study results will be shared with you if/when they become available after the entire study is completed. It is expected that this may take a number of years. The results will be shared with you based on your preferred communication method indicated in UHN's medical record system. Please talk to your study doctor if you have any questions about the results.

sections.heading: WHAT IS THE COST TO PARTICIPANTS?
complexity: Not in protocol - requires manual entry
sections.instructions: Inform the participant of any anticipated expenses associated with participation in the study.
sections.suggested_text:
<<Include if the intervention is supplied for free>>
The {{insert name(s) of product/agent/device/intervention}} will be supplied at no charge while you take part in this study.

<<Include if participants who are benefiting from the experimental intervention will continue to receive the intervention after the study is finished. Wording may be altered.>>
After the study is completed, if the study doctor feels that you are benefiting from the experimental intervention, you will continue to be provided with {{insert name(s) of product/agent/device}}.

<<If applicable, include if participants who are benefiting from the experimental intervention will NOT continue to receive the intervention after the study is finished. Wording may be altered>>
You will not be able to receive the study {{specify intervention}} after your participation in the study is completed. There are several possible reasons for this, some of which are:
• The intervention may not turn out to be effective or safe.
• The intervention may not be approved for use in Canada.
• Your caregivers may not feel it is the best option for you.

The study doctor will talk to you about your options.

<<If participation could result in additional costs, justify such costs to the REB and include an explanation of these potential costs.  Ensure that examples of extra costs are consistent with the research project>>
Taking part in this study may result in added costs to you.  For example:
• There may be costs associated with hospital visits.  For example, parking or transportation, or snacks/meals during your stay.
• You may miss work as a result of participation in this study.

<<OR If participation will not result in any costs, include the following>>
Participation in this study will not involve any additional costs to you or your private health care insurance.

sections.heading: ARE STUDY PARTICIPANTS PAID TO BE IN THIS STUDY?
complexity: Not in protocol - requires manual entry
sections.instructions: Describe compensation provided to participants, or state if no compensation is provided.
sections.suggested_text:
<<If there is no payment for participation>>
You will not be paid for taking part in this study.

<<If participants are receiving any token of appreciation>>
If you decide to participate in this study, you will receive {{specify amount and, if applicable, the interval (e.g. every three months)}}

<<If there is re-imbursement of costs for participation>>
If you decide to participate in this study, you will be reimbursed {{specify actual or maximum dollar amount}} for some study related expenses such as {{list reimbursable study-related expenses}}.

<<If receipts or other documentation is required for re-imbursement, this must be justified to the REB and must be described>>
You will need to provide your receipts for {{insert expense types e.g., parking}} to the research staff in order to be reimbursed.

sections.heading: WHAT WILL HAPPEN IF PARTICIPANT IS INJURED DURING THE STUDY?
complexity: Not in protocol - requires manual entry
sections.instructions:
Describe next steps in case of research-related injury.

This section shall not have qualifiers that suggest that a patient's rights to care could potentially be compromised in the event of study related injury and should only include the standard UHN wording.

Examples of Unacceptable Wording:
• “If you are treated according to the protocol, you will be covered by insurance held by the sponsor for medical costs arising from any study-related injury.”
• “If you suffer any side effect or other physical injury resulting directly from the study drug, the Sponsor will pay for the reasonable costs of medical treatment to the extent permitted by the law of your country if:
o You took the study drug as directed by the Study Doctor and your injury was not deliberately caused.
o The Study Doctor was immediately notified about your injury, and the medical advice of the Study Doctor was followed.”

The above statements and similar statements will not be approved for the following reasons:
• The word "protocol" is not meaningful to participants because they do not have access to the protocol.  Therefore, it is not appropriate to refer to it.
• The above statements suggest a participant’s rights to care could potentially be compromised in the event of study related injury.
• Protocol deviations may occur during a study.  Since deviations are procedures that occur "NOT according to the protocol" the language is suggesting that participants are held responsible for actions that may be outside of their control.  Therefore, in the event of a protocol deviation resulting in a negative consequence or harm to the participant, someone (not the participant) needs to be held responsible.  (That someone would be the sponsor or the site - this must be dealt with at the contract level, not the consent form level).
• Due to the nature of harm, the study doctor cannot always be notified immediately.
sections.required_text: In the case of research-related side effects or injury, medical care will be provided by {{specify e.g., your doctor; you will be referred for appropriate medical care}}.

sections.heading: COMMERCIALISATION
sections.instructions:
CONDITIONAL: Include this section only if the research could lead to commercial products (e.g., new drugs, devices, diagnostic tests). This is typically indicated when the study involves sample/data collection with commercial potential, or when the sponsor is a for-profit company developing a product.

Determine from the protocol or study context whether commercial development is a possibility. If yes, choose the appropriate sub-option:
  - Option 1: Participants will NOT receive a share of any commercial profit (most common for industry-sponsored studies)
  - Option 2: Participants WILL receive a share of commercial profits (rare — specify details)

If there is no possibility of commercialization (e.g., purely academic, government-funded observational study), omit this section.
sections.suggested_text:
<<If applicable (alter as needed to fit the research):>>
It is possible that the research conducted using your samples and/or study data may eventually lead to the development of new diagnostic tests, new drugs or devices, or other commercial products.

<<If participants will not share in commercial profit>>
There are no plans to provide payment to you if this happens.

<<If participants will share in commercial profits>>
If this happens, you will receive {{describe the participant’s share in commercial profit}}.

sections.heading: WHAT ARE THE RIGHTS OF PARTICIPANTS IN A RESEARCH STUDY?
complexity: Easy - Standard text, no mapping required
sections.required_text:
You will be told, in a timely manner, about new information that may be relevant to your willingness to stay in this study.

Your rights to privacy are legally protected by federal and provincial laws that require safeguards to ensure that your privacy is respected.

By signing this form you do not give up any of your legal rights against the study doctor, sponsor or involved institutions for compensation, nor does this form relieve the study doctor, sponsor or their agents of their legal and professional responsibilities.

sections.heading: WHAT IF RESEARCHERS DISCOVER SOMETHING ABOUT A RESEARCH PARTICIPANT?
complexity: Not in protocol - requires manual entry
sections.instructions: If incidental findings are anticipated as a result of the study, include this section and address what information will be provided to participants. Indicate the anticipated incidental findings and the anticipated management plan.
sections.suggested_text:
During the study, the researchers may learn something about you that they didn’t expect.  For example, the researchers may {{insert anticipated incidental findings e.g. find out that you have another medical condition}}.

<<Describe anticipated management plan>>
If any new clinically important information about your health is obtained as a result of your participation in this study, you will be informed.
OR
If any new information relevant to your or your relatives’ health is obtained as a result of your participation in this study, you will be given the opportunity to decide whether you wish to be made aware of that information.

sections.heading: DATA SAFETY MONITORING
complexity: Not in protocol - requires manual entry
sections.instructions:
CONDITIONAL: Include this section ONLY if the study has a Data Safety Monitoring Board (DSMB) or Data Safety Monitoring Committee (DSMC). Omit if no DSMB/DSMC is specified.

Check the protocol's 'Safety Monitoring' or 'Data Monitoring' section to confirm whether a DSMB/DSMC exists. If the protocol mentions one, determine whether the board members are employees of the sponsor or are independent (not employees of the sponsor, study doctor, or hospital). This distinction changes the {{are/are not}} placeholder in the suggested_text.
sections.suggested_text:
<<Include only if the study has a Data Safety Monitoring Board (DSMB) or Data Safety Monitoring Committee (DSMC)>>
A Data Safety Monitoring Board is a group of experts who will be reviewing the data throughout this research study to see if there are unexpected or more serious side effects than described in this form. The experts in the group {{are/are not}} employees of the sponsor, the study doctor or the hospital/clinic.

sections.heading: WHOM DO PARTICIPANTS CONTACT FOR QUESTIONS?
complexity: Not in protocol - requires manual entry
sections.instructions:
Fill in the {{insert person's name and phone number}} placeholder in the required_text with the Principal Investigator's (or designated study contact's) name and telephone number.

The REB contact details (UHN REB Chair, 416-581-7849) are standard and must not be modified.

Also determine whether a copy of the consent form will be included in the participant's health record. If yes, include the optional sentence 'A copy of the consent form that you sign to enter the study may be included in your health record/hospital chart.' at the end of the section.
sections.required_text:
If you have questions about taking part in this study, or if you suffer a research-related injury, you can talk to your study doctor, or the doctor who is in charge of the study at this institution. That person is: {{insert person's name and phone number}}.
_____________________                     _______________________
              Name                                                   Telephone Number

If you have questions about your rights as a participant or about ethical issues related to this study, call the Chair of the University Health Network Research Ethics Board (UHN REB) or the Research Ethics office number at 416-581-7849.  The REB is a group of people who oversee the ethical conduct of research studies. The UHN REB is not involved in the study at all. Everything that you discuss will be kept confidential.

You will be given a copy of this signed and dated consent form prior to participating in this study. {{include if relevant: "A copy of the consent form that you sign to enter the study may be included in your health record/hospital chart."}}
